text: Hello, I'm sharing a case of diverticulitis. This patient had a previous perforation managed by another surgeon and a drain put in. Developed a collocutaneous fistula to the drain site. Completely asymptomatic but feculent output in the drain. Sought another opinion and I recommended surgery as these types of fistulas are never going to close spontaneously.

The surgeon frees the terminal ileum from the right pelvic sidewall, reflecting the small bowel to improve access. The dissection proceeds from lateral to medial, avoiding high ligations of the vasculature. The surgeon assesses the need to take down the splenic flexure for adequate length.

The surgeon demonstrates the placement of standard ports for the sigmoid resection and proceeds with lateral dissection using scissors. As the dissection progresses towards the flexure, the surgeon switches to a vessel sealer, lifting the mesenteric fascia.

The surgeon uses a lateral approach to mobilize the splenic flexure, carefully dissecting and taking down attachments to expose the pancreas. The spleen is visible, and the colon is noted to be adhered to the splenic flexure, which is positioned high.

The surgeon performs a lateral splenic flexure release, removing attachments from the pancreas and omentum. The surgical field remains unchanged with the standard port setup for sigmoid resection. The surgeon enters the lesser sac for complete mobilization and prepares to take down the fistula, ensuring adequate length for the procedure.

The surgeon performs a lateral dissection to detach the remaining mesentery from the sidewall, using ICG to visualize the right ureteral catheters for enhanced safety due to the patient's history of perforation.
The surgeon observes the colon adhered to the abdominal wall along the track to the drain, preparing to dissect and free it from the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon medializes the mesentery, carefully hugging the bowel wall to ensure it does not affect the length of the colon during the dissection.

The surgeon begins dividing the mesentery at the previously chosen proximal margin, using multiple firings of the vessel sealer to navigate through the thickened tissue. The mesentery is bluntly dissected, creating a window to continue the division while hugging the bowel wall to minimize bleeding.

The surgeon carefully hugs the bowel wall to avoid high ligations and protect nearby nerves, minimizing mesentery bulk for extraction. Delayed bleeding is observed from the previously cut mesentery, which is controlled using bipolar cautery.
The surgeon continues dissection towards the upper rectum, periodically pausing to reassess the anatomical landmarks and ensure the correct surgical path before proceeding with the dissection.

The surgeon has cleared the mesentery on the rectal side and moves back to the proximal area to ensure the mesentery is also cleared there. They check the ICG fluorescence and mark the proximal and distal margins under white light for clarity.
The surgeon divides the bowel using scissors in cut mode, applying cautery in areas prone to bleeding. The scissors are used to minimize thermal spread compared to vessel sealers.

The surgeon divides the specimen into smaller pieces for extraction, positioning it at the rectal apex. Using a ring clamp, the surgeon carefully retrieves each piece through the rectum, ensuring no spillage or tearing occurs during the process.
The surgeon removes residual pieces of fat from the surgical field and places them into the rectum, followed by further cleaning of the area.

The surgeon prepares for a sewn end-to-end anastomosis, placing two silk sutures: one held by arm number one and another in the posterior midline to help invert the posterior lip. The posterior mucosa is carefully inverted using an over-and-over suture technique, while the anterior canal is designed to invert the anterior mucosa.

The surgeon uses three or six inch V-lock sutures, beginning in the posterior midline and stitching outward around the left side. The suturing process is visible with each bite, and the surgeon then turns to start the anterior canal.

The surgeon begins suturing at the posterior midline, taking alternating bites with the suture needle, and progresses towards the patient's right side. Care is taken to avoid covering the posterior midline to maintain visibility for the next suture placement.
The surgeon adjusts the stitch to release a caught piece of mesentery, using the same port configuration as at the start of the procedure when the splenic flexure was taken down.

So you can see if you just sort of line things up properly at the start, you can really work top to bottom along, you know, one side of the patient without too much struggle and I also do not these days use an assist port. No harm in using one at all. I just haven't been and can obviously facilitate these cases to be faster depending on what you're using your assistant for. for a task like I'm doing right now for suturing. It might be helpful for the assistant to manipulate the bowel, but I'm using arm number one for that right now and not really having too much of a trouble.

The surgeon turns the canal anteriorly over the right corner of the bowel, inspecting the anastomosis for tension. The edges of the bowel appear well vascularized, with no visible signs of char or cauterization, and the sutures are holding securely.

And what's nice in these cases is despite the amount of time that it took to morselate the specimen and extract it and then to sew this anastomosis, when I'm done this part of the procedure, I am done. All I need to do is remove the needles from the belly and I like to close the right lower quadrant port because I think that that one is at risk for hernia. Then essentially you're done. So although my intuitive app tells me how slow I am with my anterior resections compared to the national average, I know that that's the console time, but the total OR time might be around the same. And again, this is all just my personal preference. I am currently not working with anybody in the operating room with me. who is a learner other than medical students. And so I am currently in a phase of doing a lot of these because I think it's a really nice way to do this operation. And sure enough, this was a same day discharge and I really didn't have any concerns for the patient who did really nicely.

The surgeon brings the two sutures from both corners around anteriorly to meet at the anterior midline, ensuring symmetry in the suturing. The surgeon performs a leak test to check for any gaps in the corners or anteriorly, using a three-oh stitch if necessary.

The surgeon drives a nineteen millimeter practice scope through the anastomosis, observing no bubbles, indicating a successful leak test. The needles and sponges are removed, and the suture passer is used to close the right lower quadrant eight millimeter port to prevent hernia formation. The surgeon inspects the area towards the splenic flexure to clear any effluent.
